Apple___Apple_scab.name: Apple Scab
Apple___Apple_scab.symptoms: Dark, scaly lesions on leaves and fruit. Olive-green to black spots that turn corky over time. Premature leaf drop and misshapen, cracked fruits.
Apple___Apple_scab.organic: Apply neem oil spray every 7–10 days. Use sulfur-based fungicide. Remove and destroy fallen leaves promptly.
Apple___Apple_scab.chemical: Apply captan or mancozeb fungicide at 7–10 day intervals. Use myclobutanil during early season for systemic protection.
Apple___Apple_scab.prevention: Plant resistant apple varieties. Prune trees for good air circulation. Avoid overhead irrigation. Rake and destroy fallen leaves each autumn.

Apple___Black_rot.name: Apple Black Rot
Apple___Black_rot.symptoms: Circular brown lesions with purple margins on leaves. Black, mummified fruit with concentric rings. Cankers with reddish-brown discoloration on bark.
Apple___Black_rot.organic: Remove and burn mummified fruits and infected branches. Apply copper-based fungicide. Maintain proper sanitation around the tree.
Apple___Black_rot.chemical: Apply thiophanate-methyl or captan at pink bud through second cover. Remove all mummified fruits before applying sprays.
Apple___Black_rot.prevention: Prune infected branches at least 15 cm below visible symptoms. Avoid wounding trees. Dispose of all pruning debris away from the orchard.

Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.name: Cedar Apple Rust
Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.symptoms: Bright orange-yellow spots on upper leaf surface, with tube-like structures on the underside. Infected fruit may be deformed and drop early.
Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.organic: Remove nearby cedar/juniper host trees if possible. Apply sulfur or neem oil during wet spring weather.
Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.chemical: Apply myclobutanil or propiconazole fungicides from pink bud stage through petal fall. Repeat every 7–10 days during wet weather.
Apple___Cedar_apple_rust.prevention: Plant rust-resistant apple varieties. Remove cedar/juniper trees within 300 meters. Apply fungicides preventively before wet weather periods.

Apple___healthy.name: Healthy Apple
Apple___healthy.symptoms: No disease detected. The plant appears healthy.
Apple___healthy.organic: Continue regular neem oil applications as a preventive measure.
Apple___healthy.chemical: No treatment needed. Maintain a regular spray schedule as prevention.
Apple___healthy.prevention: Maintain good pruning practices, balanced fertilization, and proper irrigation.

Blueberry___healthy.name: Healthy Blueberry
Blueberry___healthy.symptoms: No disease detected. The plant appears healthy.
Blueberry___healthy.organic: Apply mulch to maintain soil moisture and prevent weeds.
Blueberry___healthy.prevention: Maintain soil pH between 4.5–5.5. Ensure good drainage and air circulation.

Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.name: Cherry Powdery Mildew
Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.symptoms: White powdery coating on young leaves, shoots, and fruit. Infected leaves curl and distort. Severe infections stunt growth and reduce fruit quality.
Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.organic: Spray diluted baking soda solution (1 tbsp per liter). Apply neem oil or potassium bicarbonate. Improve air circulation through pruning.
Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.chemical: Apply sulfur-based fungicides or trifloxystrobin. Avoid using sulfur when temperatures exceed 32°C.
Cherry_(including_sour)___Powdery_mildew.prevention: Plant resistant varieties. Prune for open canopy structure. Avoid excess nitrogen fertilization. Water at the base, not overhead.

Cherry_(including_sour)___healthy.name: Healthy Cherry
Cherry_(including_sour)___healthy.symptoms: No disease detected. The plant appears healthy.
Cherry_(including_sour)___healthy.organic: Apply neem oil preventively during humid weather.
Cherry_(including_sour)___healthy.prevention: Prune annually to maintain air flow. Avoid overwatering.

Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.name: Corn Gray Leaf Spot
Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.symptoms: Rectangular tan-to-gray lesions with yellow borders, running parallel to leaf veins. Lesions merge and kill large areas of leaf tissue in severe cases.
Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.organic: Rotate crops — avoid planting corn in the same field two years in a row. Till infected residue to reduce fungal spores.
Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.chemical: Apply strobilurin or triazole fungicides at early tasseling stage. Products containing azoxystrobin or pyraclostrobin are effective.
Corn_(maize)___Cercospora_leaf_spot Gray.prevention: Use resistant hybrids. Practice 2-year crop rotation. Manage crop residue by plowing it under after harvest.

Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.name: Corn Common Rust
Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.symptoms: Small, oval to elongated, brick-red pustules scattered on both leaf surfaces. Pustules rupture and release powdery reddish-brown spores. Severe infections cause premature leaf death.
Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.organic: Plant resistant hybrid varieties. Remove and destroy severely infected plants early.
Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.chemical: Apply fungicides containing triazole or strobilurin (e.g., propiconazole, azoxystrobin) when pustules first appear. One application is usually sufficient.
Corn_(maize)___Common_rust_.prevention: Use rust-resistant corn hybrids. Scout fields early and act quickly. Avoid late planting dates that expose plants during vulnerable growth stages.

Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.name: Corn Northern Leaf Blight
Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.symptoms: Large, cigar-shaped gray-green or tan lesions 2–15 cm long on leaves. Lesions may cover entire leaves and lead to premature death of the plant.
Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.organic: Rotate with non-host crops for at least 1 year. Incorporate infected residue to reduce inoculum.
Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.chemical: Apply strobilurin-based fungicides (azoxystrobin, picoxystrobin) at silking stage. Triazole fungicides also provide good control.
Corn_(maize)___Northern_Leaf_Blight.prevention: Plant resistant or tolerant hybrids. Practice 2-year crop rotation. Manage surface residue after harvest.

Corn_(maize)___healthy.name: Healthy Corn
Corn_(maize)___healthy.symptoms: No disease detected. The plant appears healthy.
Corn_(maize)___healthy.organic: Maintain balanced fertilization with adequate potassium.
Corn_(maize)___healthy.prevention: Rotate crops and use disease-resistant hybrids each season.

Grape___Black_rot.name: Grape Black Rot
Grape___Black_rot.symptoms: Small, circular tan spots with dark borders on leaves. Infected berries turn brown, then black and wrinkle into hard mummified fruit.
Grape___Black_rot.organic: Remove and destroy all mummified fruit and infected shoots. Apply copper-based fungicides early in the season.
Grape___Black_rot.chemical: Apply myclobutanil, mancozeb, or captan fungicides from bud break through veraison. Protect new growth every 7–14 days.
Grape___Black_rot.prevention: Remove all mummified fruit before budbreak. Ensure good air circulation in the canopy. Avoid overhead irrigation.

Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).name: Grape Esca (Black Measles)
Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).symptoms: Tiger-stripe pattern on leaves (yellow/red margins with green veins). Berries develop dark spots (measles). Severe cases cause sudden vine collapse called apoplexy.
Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).organic: Prune infected wood and seal wounds immediately with pruning paste. Remove and burn severely infected vines.
Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).chemical: No fully effective chemical cure exists. Trunk injections with sodium arsenite (restricted) were used historically. Focus on prevention.
Grape___Esca_(Black_Measles).prevention: Protect pruning wounds with fungicidal paste containing trichoderma. Prune during dry weather. Minimize large pruning cuts.

Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.name: Grape Leaf Blight
Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.symptoms: Irregular dark brown spots with yellow halos on older leaves. Spots enlarge and leaves turn yellow and drop. Reduces photosynthesis and fruit quality.
Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.organic: Apply copper-based sprays. Remove infected leaves from the ground. Improve vine canopy airflow.
Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.chemical: Apply mancozeb or copper oxychloride fungicides. Begin sprays when symptoms first appear and repeat every 14 days.
Grape___Leaf_blight_(Isariopsis_Leaf_Spo.prevention: Maintain open vine canopy. Avoid waterlogging. Practice balanced nutrition. Remove fallen infected leaves.

Grape___healthy.name: Healthy Grape
Grape___healthy.symptoms: No disease detected. The vine appears healthy.
Grape___healthy.organic: Apply preventive copper sprays before wet seasons.
Grape___healthy.prevention: Maintain open canopy, good drainage, and balanced fertilization.

Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).name: Citrus Greening (HLB)
Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).symptoms: Asymmetric yellowing (blotchy mottle) of leaves. Small, lopsided, bitter fruit that remains green at maturity. Twig dieback and progressive tree decline.
Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).organic: No cure exists. Remove infected trees to prevent spread. Manage the Asian citrus psyllid vector using insecticidal soap.
Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).chemical: Control the psyllid vector with systemic insecticides (imidacloprid, thiamethoxam). No chemical can cure HLB once a tree is infected.
Orange___Haunglongbing_(Citrus_greening).prevention: Use certified disease-free planting material. Control psyllid populations aggressively. Remove and destroy infected trees immediately to protect neighbors.

Peach___Bacterial_spot.name: Peach Bacterial Spot
Peach___Bacterial_spot.symptoms: Small, water-soaked angular spots on leaves that turn purple or brown with yellow halos. Spots may drop out leaving 'shot-hole' appearance. Fruit develops sunken, dark lesions.
Peach___Bacterial_spot.organic: Apply copper-based bactericides in spring. Avoid overhead irrigation. Remove severely infected plant material.
Peach___Bacterial_spot.chemical: Apply copper hydroxide or oxytetracycline antibiotics starting at bud break. Repeat every 5–7 days during wet weather.
Peach___Bacterial_spot.prevention: Plant resistant peach varieties. Prune to improve air circulation. Avoid excessive nitrogen. Do not work with wet plants.

Peach___healthy.name: Healthy Peach
Peach___healthy.symptoms: No disease detected. The plant appears healthy.
Peach___healthy.organic: Apply preventive copper sprays before wet weather.
Peach___healthy.prevention: Prune regularly for air circulation. Apply balanced fertilizer and adequate irrigation.

Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.name: Bell Pepper Bacterial Spot
Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.symptoms: Small, water-soaked spots on leaves and fruit that enlarge and turn brown with yellow halos. Leaves may drop, exposing fruit to sunscald. Fruit develops raised, scabby lesions.
Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.organic: Apply copper-based bactericides. Avoid working in fields when plants are wet. Remove and destroy infected plant debris.
Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.chemical: Apply copper hydroxide plus mancozeb mixture. Begin at first symptom and repeat every 5–7 days during rainy weather.
Pepper,_bell___Bacterial_spot.prevention: Use certified disease-free seed. Practice 2-year crop rotation. Avoid overhead irrigation. Stake plants for better air circulation.

Pepper,_bell___healthy.name: Healthy Bell Pepper
Pepper,_bell___healthy.symptoms: No disease detected. The plant appears healthy.
Pepper,_bell___healthy.organic: Apply neem oil to prevent common fungal issues.
Pepper,_bell___healthy.prevention: Ensure proper spacing, avoid waterlogging, and rotate crops annually.

Potato___Early_blight.name: Potato Early Blight
Potato___Early_blight.symptoms: Dark brown to black concentric ring spots (target board pattern) on older leaves. Yellow halo around lesions. Lesions enlarge and leaves die from the bottom up.
Potato___Early_blight.organic: Apply neem oil or copper-based fungicide. Remove infected lower leaves. Ensure proper plant nutrition — stressed plants are more susceptible.
Potato___Early_blight.chemical: Apply chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or azoxystrobin. Begin when lesions first appear and repeat every 7–10 days.
Potato___Early_blight.prevention: Plant certified disease-free seed potatoes. Maintain adequate nitrogen and potassium. Avoid waterlogged conditions. Practice 3-year crop rotation.

Potato___Late_blight.name: Potato Late Blight
Potato___Late_blight.symptoms: Large, irregular, water-soaked dark-green to brown lesions on leaves and stems. White fuzzy sporulation on leaf undersides in humid conditions. Tubers show reddish-brown dry rot.
Potato___Late_blight.organic: Apply copper-based fungicides preventively. Destroy volunteer potato plants. Remove infected plants immediately to prevent spread.
Potato___Late_blight.chemical: Apply systemic fungicides: metalaxyl, mefenoxam, or dimethomorph. Begin before symptoms appear during high-risk weather. Alternate chemistries to prevent resistance.
Potato___Late_blight.prevention: Use resistant varieties. Plant certified seed. Destroy crop debris after harvest. Avoid excessive irrigation. Monitor closely during cool, humid weather.

Potato___healthy.name: Healthy Potato
Potato___healthy.symptoms: No disease detected. The plant appears healthy.
Potato___healthy.organic: Apply preventive copper sprays during humid periods.
Potato___healthy.prevention: Use certified seed, maintain crop rotation, and hill soil around plants.

Raspberry___healthy.name: Healthy Raspberry
Raspberry___healthy.symptoms: No disease detected. The plant appears healthy.
Raspberry___healthy.organic: Mulch around canes to retain moisture and suppress weeds.
Raspberry___healthy.prevention: Remove old canes after fruiting. Ensure good drainage and air flow.

Soybean___healthy.name: Healthy Soybean
Soybean___healthy.symptoms: No disease detected. The plant appears healthy.
Soybean___healthy.organic: Inoculate seeds with Rhizobium bacteria to enhance nitrogen fixation.
Soybean___healthy.prevention: Rotate with non-legume crops. Monitor for soybean cyst nematode in susceptible fields.

Squash___Powdery_mildew.name: Squash Powdery Mildew
Squash___Powdery_mildew.symptoms: White powdery patches on upper and lower leaf surfaces. Infected leaves yellow, dry out, and die. Reduces fruit set and causes early plant senescence.
Squash___Powdery_mildew.organic: Spray diluted milk (40% milk, 60% water) or baking soda solution. Apply neem oil or potassium bicarbonate every 7 days.
Squash___Powdery_mildew.chemical: Apply sulfur, trifloxystrobin, or tebuconazole fungicides at first sign. Avoid sulfur sprays in temperatures above 32°C.
Squash___Powdery_mildew.prevention: Plant resistant squash varieties. Space plants widely for air circulation. Water at the base in the morning. Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization.

Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.name: Strawberry Leaf Scorch
Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.symptoms: Small, irregular purple to dark brown spots on leaf surfaces. Spots coalesce causing leaves to look 'scorched'. Severely infected leaves turn red-purple and die.
Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.organic: Remove and destroy infected leaves. Apply copper-based fungicides after harvest. Maintain good air circulation around plants.
Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.chemical: Apply captan or myclobutanil fungicides. Begin sprays in early spring and continue through the growing season as needed.
Strawberry___Leaf_scorch.prevention: Plant certified disease-free runners. Avoid overhead irrigation. Remove old leaves after harvest. Renovate beds every 2–3 years.

Strawberry___healthy.name: Healthy Strawberry
Strawberry___healthy.symptoms: No disease detected. The plant appears healthy.
Strawberry___healthy.organic: Apply neem oil preventively before humid weather.
Strawberry___healthy.prevention: Renovate beds after harvest. Use certified disease-free planting material.

Tomato___Bacterial_spot.name: Tomato Bacterial Spot
Tomato___Bacterial_spot.symptoms: Small, water-soaked spots on leaves that turn dark brown with yellow halos. Spots may drop out creating 'shot-holes'. Fruit develops raised, scabby spots reducing marketability.
Tomato___Bacterial_spot.organic: Apply copper-based bactericides every 7 days. Remove infected leaves. Avoid handling plants when wet.
Tomato___Bacterial_spot.chemical: Apply copper hydroxide plus mancozeb mixture. Start sprays at transplanting and continue through season during wet weather.
Tomato___Bacterial_spot.prevention: Use disease-free certified seeds. Rotate tomato fields every 3 years. Stake plants and prune for airflow. Avoid overhead irrigation.

Tomato___Early_blight.name: Tomato Early Blight
Tomato___Early_blight.symptoms: Dark concentric ring (bullseye) spots on older, lower leaves first. Yellow tissue surrounds the lesions. Severe infections cause defoliation and sunscald on fruit.
Tomato___Early_blight.organic: Remove infected lower leaves promptly. Apply neem oil or copper fungicide weekly. Mulch to prevent soil splash.
Tomato___Early_blight.chemical: Apply chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or azoxystrobin. Begin at first sign and repeat every 7–10 days. Alternate fungicides to prevent resistance.
Tomato___Early_blight.prevention: Use resistant varieties. Stake plants. Mulch around base. Rotate crops every 3 years. Water at soil level, not overhead.

Tomato___Late_blight.name: Tomato Late Blight
Tomato___Late_blight.symptoms: Large, irregular water-soaked dark-green to gray lesions on leaves and stems. White sporulation on leaf undersides in cool, humid weather. Fruit develops dark, greasy-looking rot.
Tomato___Late_blight.organic: Apply copper-based fungicides preventively. Remove infected plants immediately. Do not compost infected material.
Tomato___Late_blight.chemical: Apply metalaxyl, mefenoxam, or fluopicolide. Start preventive sprays during cool, humid weather. Alternate chemistries every 5–7 days.
Tomato___Late_blight.prevention: Plant resistant varieties. Destroy crop debris. Avoid overhead watering. Increase plant spacing. Monitor closely during cool, wet conditions.

Tomato___Leaf_Mold.name: Tomato Leaf Mold
Tomato___Leaf_Mold.symptoms: Pale greenish-yellow spots on upper leaf surface with olive-green to grayish-brown velvety mold on the underside. Lower leaves affected first. Severe infections cause leaf curl and drop.
Tomato___Leaf_Mold.organic: Reduce greenhouse humidity below 85%. Improve ventilation. Apply copper or neem-based fungicides.
Tomato___Leaf_Mold.chemical: Apply chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or copper-based fungicides. Applications every 7–14 days are effective when started early.
Tomato___Leaf_Mold.prevention: Maintain greenhouse relative humidity below 85%. Stake and prune tomatoes for airflow. Water in the morning. Use resistant varieties for greenhouse production.

Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.name: Tomato Septoria Leaf Spot
Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.symptoms: Numerous small, circular spots with dark brown borders and gray-white centers on lower leaves. Tiny black dots (pycnidia) visible inside spots. Causes severe defoliation working upward.
Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.organic: Remove infected lower leaves as soon as spots appear. Mulch soil to prevent splash dispersal. Apply copper-based fungicides.
Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.chemical: Apply chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or azoxystrobin fungicides. Start at first symptom and repeat every 7–10 days during wet conditions.
Tomato___Septoria_leaf_spot.prevention: Rotate crops for 2–3 years. Stake plants. Avoid wetting foliage. Mulch soil. Remove and destroy all crop debris at season end.

Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.name: Tomato Spider Mites
Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.symptoms: Fine stippling or bronzing of leaves, starting on lower leaves. Fine webbing visible on undersides of leaves in heavy infestations. Leaves turn yellow, dry up, and drop.
Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.organic: Spray plants with strong water jet to knock off mites. Apply neem oil or insecticidal soap to leaf undersides every 3–5 days. Introduce predatory mites (Phytoseiulus persimilis).
Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.chemical: Apply abamectin, bifenazate, or spiromesifen miticides. Rotate chemistries to prevent resistance. Target leaf undersides.
Tomato___Spider_mites Two-spotted_spider.prevention: Avoid plant stress — keep plants well-watered and fertilized. Avoid dusty conditions. Monitor regularly and act early. Avoid broad-spectrum insecticides that kill natural predators.

Tomato___Target_Spot.name: Tomato Target Spot
Tomato___Target_Spot.symptoms: Dark brown lesions with concentric rings (target pattern) on leaves, stems, and fruit. Yellow halos around lesions. Causes premature defoliation and reduces yield.
Tomato___Target_Spot.organic: Remove infected leaves. Improve plant spacing for better air circulation. Apply copper-based fungicides preventively.
Tomato___Target_Spot.chemical: Apply chlorothalonil, azoxystrobin, or boscalid fungicides at 7–14 day intervals. Start when conditions favor disease development.
Tomato___Target_Spot.prevention: Plant resistant varieties. Stake plants and prune lower leaves. Rotate crops. Avoid overhead irrigation. Maintain adequate plant nutrition.

Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.name: Tomato Yellow Leaf Curl Virus
Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.symptoms: Leaves curl upward and inward with yellowing margins. Severe stunting and reduced fruit set. Plants infected early may produce no marketable fruit.
Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.organic: No cure once infected. Remove and destroy infected plants immediately. Control whitefly vector with yellow sticky traps and reflective mulch.
Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.chemical: Control the whitefly (Bemisia tabaci) vector with imidacloprid or thiamethoxam as soil drench or foliar spray. No chemical cures the virus itself.
Tomato___Tomato_Yellow_Leaf_Curl_Virus.prevention: Use TYLCV-resistant tomato varieties. Manage whitefly populations with systemic insecticides at transplanting. Use insect-proof net houses. Remove infected plants promptly.

Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.name: Tomato Mosaic Virus
Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.symptoms: Mosaic pattern of light and dark green on leaves. Leaves may be mottled, distorted, or fernleaf-like. Fruit may show yellow streaks or brown discoloration internally.
Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.organic: No cure. Remove and destroy infected plants. Wash hands and tools with soap before handling healthy plants. Do not use tobacco products near plants.
Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.chemical: No effective chemical treatment. Focus on prevention and elimination of infected plant material.
Tomato___Tomato_mosaic_virus.prevention: Use certified virus-free seeds or resistant varieties. Disinfect tools with 10% bleach solution. Do not touch healthy plants after handling infected ones. Control aphid vectors.

Tomato___healthy.name: Healthy Tomato
Tomato___healthy.symptoms: No disease detected. The plant appears healthy.
Tomato___healthy.organic: Apply neem oil preventively every 2 weeks during the growing season.
Tomato___healthy.prevention: Stake plants, rotate crops every 3 years, mulch soil, and water at the base.
